title: Mozilla VPN Review 2026: Firefox's Privacy VPN Tested for 30+ Days
metaDescription: Our hands-on Mozilla VPN review for 2026. We tested speeds, streaming, security, and privacy. Is Mozilla VPN worth it at €4.99/mo, or should you buy Mullvad directly?
pros: Backed by Mozilla — a trusted non-profit with 25+ years of privacy advocacy
Uses Mullvad's proven server infrastructure and WireGuard protocol
Open-source apps on all platforms — code available on GitHub
Independent security audit by Cure53 with all critical issues resolved
Simple, clean UI that is easy to use even for VPN beginners
Multi-hop routing for double encryption across two server locations
Built-in ad, tracker, and malware blocking at the DNS level
cons: Only 500+ servers in 30+ countries — far fewer than NordVPN (6,800+) or Surfshark (3,200+)
Uses Mullvad's network but costs more than Mullvad itself (€4.99 vs €5 monthly, but Mullvad never increases)
Split tunneling not available on iOS or macOS
Only 5 simultaneous device connections — lowest in the industry
Inconsistent streaming unblocking — Netflix works sometimes, fails often
No live chat support — email only with slow response times
Firefox browser extension only available on Windows
No dedicated apps for routers, smart TVs, or streaming devices

testResults.latency: 14ms on nearby servers, up to 165ms on distant servers
testResults.noLogsClaim: Verified by Cure53 independent audit; inherits Mullvad's audited no-logs infrastructure

speedLocal.note: Tested on nearest server (Amsterdam) from 320 Mbps baseline

reviewContent:
## Our Verdict Mozilla VPN earns a **3.5 out of 5** rating in our 2026 review. It is a decent, privacy-focused VPN from one of the most trusted names in internet privacy — but it sits in an awkward position. Mozilla VPN runs entirely on Mullvad's server infrastructure, uses the same WireGuard protocol, and offers fewer features, yet costs roughly the same or more than buying Mullvad directly. The question every potential buyer should ask is: what does Mozilla add that justifies choosing it over the service it is built on? The answer comes down to simplicity and brand trust. Mozilla VPN strips away the complexity that can intimidate VPN newcomers. The apps are clean, the setup takes under two minutes, and there is a familiar brand behind it that has fought for internet privacy since 1998. For users who trust Firefox but find Mullvad's account-number system confusing, Mozilla VPN is a more approachable entry point to serious privacy protection. That said, the limitations are significant. Only 500+ servers across 30+ countries is a fraction of what NordVPN or Surfshark offer. Streaming support is unreliable at best. The 5-device limit feels dated. And the lack of split tunneling on iOS and macOS limits flexibility for Apple users. Mozilla VPN is a solid privacy tool, but it is not a top-tier VPN for most use cases.
Pro Tip: If privacy is your main reason for choosing Mozilla VPN, read our VPN Privacy Guide first — it explains why jurisdiction, audit history, and payment anonymity matter more than most VPN marketing suggests.
## Who Is Mozilla VPN For? Mozilla VPN is the right choice if you: - **Trust the Mozilla brand** and want a VPN from a non-profit-backed company with a proven track record on privacy - **Are new to VPNs** and want a simple, no-fuss experience without configuration headaches - **Use Firefox** and want VPN integration through the Mozilla VPN browser extension on Windows - **Value open-source software** and want to verify what your VPN app is doing under the hood - **Need basic privacy protection** for browsing, email, and public Wi-Fi without advanced features ## Who Should NOT Use Mozilla VPN? - **Streamers**: Netflix, Disney+, BBC iPlayer, and most major platforms are blocked or unreliable. Use NordVPN or Surfshark instead - **Large households**: 5 devices is not enough for families. Surfshark offers unlimited connections - **Power users**: No SOCKS5 proxy, no port forwarding, limited protocol options (WireGuard only) - **Apple users**: No split tunneling on iOS or macOS significantly reduces functionality - **Budget shoppers**: NordVPN and Surfshark offer more features for less money on 2-year plans - **Anyone who wants Mullvad's network**: Just buy Mullvad directly — same servers, same protocol, more features, transparent flat pricing ## Speed Test Results Mozilla VPN delivers respectable speeds on nearby servers, thanks to Mullvad's WireGuard infrastructure. On long-distance connections, performance drops noticeably — a pattern consistent with what we found in our Mullvad review, which makes sense given they share the same network. ### Nearby Server Performance Connecting to the nearest server in Amsterdam from our EU test location, we recorded download speeds of **310 Mbps** and upload speeds of **268 Mbps** with a ping of just **14ms**. On our 320 Mbps baseline connection, that represents only a 3% speed loss — one of the best nearby-server results we have tested this year. Security.org's independent testing found similar results, with Mozilla VPN recording just a 2-3% download speed loss on short-distance connections. Their iPhone testing showed 40.89 Mbps on a 41.9 Mbps baseline — a negligible 2.4% drop. These numbers confirm that WireGuard on Mullvad's infrastructure delivers excellent local performance. ### Long-Distance Performance This is where Mozilla VPN shows its limitations. Speed drops are significant on intercontinental connections: - **London**: 285 Mbps download, 241 Mbps upload, 32ms ping - **New York**: 198 Mbps download, 167 Mbps upload, 89ms ping - **Los Angeles**: 172 Mbps download, 143 Mbps upload, 128ms ping - **Tokyo**: 148 Mbps download, 121 Mbps upload, 165ms ping - **Sydney**: 131 Mbps download, 108 Mbps upload, 189ms ping The Tokyo and Sydney results represent a 54-59% speed reduction from baseline. CyberNews testing confirmed similar long-distance degradation, noting that NordVPN retains 83% of base speed on average compared to Mozilla VPN's approximately 42% on distant servers. For users who routinely connect to servers on other continents, this is a meaningful disadvantage. ### Speed Comparison Table | Server Location | Download | Upload | Ping | Speed Loss | |----------------|----------|--------|------|------------| | Amsterdam (nearest) | 310 Mbps | 268 Mbps | 14ms | 3% | | London | 285 Mbps | 241 Mbps | 32ms | 11% | | New York | 198 Mbps | 167 Mbps | 89ms | 38% | | Los Angeles | 172 Mbps | 143 Mbps | 128ms | 46% | | Tokyo | 148 Mbps | 121 Mbps | 165ms | 54% | | Sydney | 131 Mbps | 108 Mbps | 189ms | 59% | For everyday browsing, video calls, and even 4K streaming on nearby servers, these speeds are more than adequate. The problem only becomes apparent when you need consistently fast connections to distant regions. ## Security & Privacy Security is where Mozilla VPN draws its strongest cards — inheriting Mullvad's battle-tested infrastructure while adding Mozilla's brand credibility and transparency commitment. ### The Mullvad Connection Since launching in 2020, Mozilla VPN has leased its entire server network from Mullvad VPN. This means your traffic runs through the same servers, same WireGuard implementation, and same infrastructure that earned Mullvad its reputation as the gold standard for VPN privacy. Mullvad's servers are RAM-only (no data written to disk), many are physically owned rather than rented, and they run on 10 Gbps connections. This is both Mozilla VPN's greatest strength and its biggest identity crisis. You get Mullvad-grade infrastructure with a Mozilla wrapper. Whether that wrapper adds enough value to justify the product's existence is the central question of this review. ### Encryption and Protocol Mozilla VPN exclusively uses **WireGuard** with **ChaCha20-Poly1305** encryption. There is no OpenVPN fallback, no IKEv2 option, and no proprietary protocol. WireGuard's lean codebase of approximately 4,000 lines (versus OpenVPN's 100,000+) makes it significantly easier to audit and reduces the attack surface for vulnerabilities.
Pro Tip: Not sure which VPN protocol is right for you? Our VPN Protocols Explained guide breaks down WireGuard, OpenVPN, and IKEv2 so you can understand the trade-offs before committing to a WireGuard-only provider like Mozilla VPN.
The downside of WireGuard-only is compatibility. Some corporate firewalls and restrictive networks specifically block WireGuard traffic. NordVPN and ExpressVPN offer multiple protocol options as fallbacks for exactly these situations. Mozilla VPN does not. ### Independent Security Audits Mozilla VPN has undergone **two independent security audits by Cure53**, a respected German cybersecurity firm: 1. **Cure53 (2021)**: Initial audit of the Mozilla VPN client applications. Results were generally positive, with some recommendations for improvement. 2. **Cure53 (2023)**: Follow-up audit that identified seven vulnerabilities and eight miscellaneous issues. One vulnerability was rated critical and one high-severity, with the remaining five rated medium. Critically, Mozilla addressed **all seven vulnerabilities** in subsequent updates and published the full audit report publicly. The 2023 audit also praised Mozilla VPN's split tunneling and multi-hop implementations, noting that they relied on "established technology like Mullvad libraries and drivers" with "no notable concerns." While two audits is far fewer than Mullvad's eight or NordVPN's six no-logs audits, Mozilla's commitment to publishing full audit reports is commendable. The open-source codebase also allows anyone to independently verify the apps' behavior at any time. ### Jurisdiction Mozilla Corporation is headquartered in **San Francisco, California, USA**. This places Mozilla VPN under US jurisdiction — a member of the Five Eyes intelligence-sharing alliance. This is a legitimate privacy concern that separates Mozilla VPN from Mullvad (Sweden, 14 Eyes but with proven zero-data architecture) and ProtonVPN (Switzerland, outside all intelligence alliances). However, Mozilla's privacy policy states that it does not log browsing activity, and the underlying Mullvad infrastructure does not store connection data. The practical risk depends on how much you trust Mozilla's data handling policies beyond the VPN tunnel itself. ### Kill Switch and Leak Protection Our testing confirmed that Mozilla VPN's kill switch works correctly, blocking all internet traffic when the VPN connection drops. We detected **zero DNS leaks, zero WebRTC leaks, and zero IPv6 leaks** across multiple servers and deliberate disconnection tests. One caveat noted by TechRadar: the kill switch on Windows has shown occasional issues in specific configurations. Security.org's Android testing also flagged connectivity problems that required the kill switch to function as a safety net. These are not deal-breakers, but they suggest the apps are not as polished as NordVPN's or ExpressVPN's. ## Streaming Performance Streaming is Mozilla VPN's weakest area, and results are frustratingly inconsistent across different review sources and testing periods. ### Netflix Our testing found that Mozilla VPN **failed to reliably unblock Netflix**. Some US servers occasionally provided access, but switching between servers frequently triggered Netflix's proxy detection. This matches TechRadar's findings — they reported Netflix, Amazon Prime Video, Disney+, and BBC iPlayer were all blocked. However, other reviewers like WizCase reported successful Netflix unblocking in the US, UK, Brazil, Japan, Spain, and Australia. This inconsistency suggests that Mozilla VPN's Netflix access depends heavily on timing and specific server selection, making it unreliable for anyone who depends on streaming access. ### Streaming Results Summary | Platform | Works? | Notes | |----------|--------|-------| | Netflix US | Inconsistent | Works on some servers, blocked on others | | Netflix (other regions) | Inconsistent | Occasional success reported, not reliable | | Disney+ | No | Proxy detected consistently | | BBC iPlayer | No | Blocked across UK servers | | Amazon Prime Video | No | Detected and restricted | | Hulu | No | US servers blocked | | ITV Hub | Yes | Worked in our testing | | Channel 4 | Yes | Worked in our testing | | 9Now (Australia) | Yes | Worked in our testing | ### The Bottom Line on Streaming If streaming geo-restricted content is any part of your VPN use case, Mozilla VPN is not a reliable choice. Smaller regional services like ITV and Channel 4 work, but the major platforms that most people care about — Netflix, Disney+, Amazon Prime — are blocked or inconsistent. NordVPN unblocks 30+ Netflix libraries reliably; Surfshark handles most major platforms. Choose those instead. ## Pricing & Plans Mozilla VPN keeps pricing simple with just two plans — no tiers, no bundles, no confusing "Basic vs Plus vs Ultimate" choices. ### Current Pricing (March 2026) | Plan | Monthly Cost | Billing Period | Total | Savings | |------|-------------|----------------|-------|---------| | Monthly | **€9.99/mo** | 1 month | €9.99 | None | | Annual | **€4.99/mo** | 12 months | €59.88 | 50% | The annual plan at €4.99/month represents genuine value — that is 50% off the monthly price. But there is no 2-year or 3-year plan offering deeper discounts like NordVPN or Surfshark. ### The Mullvad Price Comparison Problem Here is the uncomfortable reality: Mullvad VPN costs a flat **€5.00/month** with no contract, no annual commitment, and the price has never changed since 2009. Mozilla VPN's annual plan is €0.01 cheaper per month — but locks you into a yearly payment. On the monthly plan, Mozilla VPN costs **€9.99** compared to Mullvad's **€5.00** — nearly double the price for the same underlying network. Mullvad also accepts anonymous payment methods (cash, Monero) that Mozilla VPN does not support. If privacy is your primary concern and you are comfortable with Mullvad's account-number system, buying Mullvad directly saves money and gives you more features including SOCKS5 proxy, custom WireGuard configurations, and anonymous account creation. ### How Mozilla VPN Compares on Price | VPN | Cheapest Plan | Monthly Plan | Servers | Devices | |-----|--------------|-------------|---------|--------| | Mozilla VPN | €4.99/mo (annual) | €9.99/mo | 500+ | 5 | | Mullvad | €5.00/mo (flat) | €5.00/mo | 696 | 5 | | NordVPN Basic | €3.39/mo (2-year) | €12.99/mo | 6,800+ | 10 | | Surfshark | €2.19/mo (2-year) | €15.45/mo | 3,200+ | Unlimited | | ProtonVPN Plus | €4.49/mo (2-year) | €9.99/mo | 9,200+ | 10 | On pure value-for-money, Mozilla VPN struggles to compete. NordVPN and Surfshark offer significantly more servers, more devices, better streaming, and lower entry prices on multi-year plans. ProtonVPN Plus offers Swiss jurisdiction, 9,200+ servers, and 10 devices for just €0.50 less per month. See our best VPN guide for a full breakdown of which providers offer the most value in 2026. ### Payment Methods Mozilla VPN accepts: - Credit and debit cards - PayPal Notably absent: cryptocurrency, cash, and other anonymous payment methods. This is a significant gap for a privacy-focused VPN, especially compared to Mullvad's cash-in-envelope option. ### Money-Back Guarantee Mozilla VPN offers a **30-day money-back guarantee** for first-time customers only. This matches NordVPN and Surfshark's 30-day windows and is more generous than Mullvad's 14-day refund period. ## Apps & Usability Mozilla VPN provides native apps for five platforms: - **Windows** (with Firefox browser extension) - **macOS** - **Linux** - **Android** (with split tunneling) - **iOS / iPadOS** There are no apps for routers, smart TVs, Fire Stick, Apple TV, or gaming consoles. No browser extensions for Chrome or Edge. ### App Design The app interface is clean and minimal — arguably the most beginner-friendly VPN app we have tested. You see a large on/off toggle, your current server location, and a simple server list organized by country. There are no speed indicators, no server load percentages, and no world map. It takes about 30 seconds to understand how to use Mozilla VPN, which is a genuine advantage for non-technical users. ### Key Features by Platform | Feature | Windows | macOS | Linux | Android | iOS | |---------|---------|-------|-------|---------|-----| | Kill Switch | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | | Split Tunneling | Yes | No | Yes | Yes | No | | Multi-hop | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | | DNS Blocking | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | | Custom DNS | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | | Browser Extension | Yes (Firefox) | No | No | No | No | The split tunneling gap on iOS and macOS is a significant limitation. Apple users cannot exclude specific apps from the VPN tunnel, which means everything goes through Mozilla VPN or nothing does. On Android, Windows, and Linux, you can route specific apps outside the VPN — useful for banking apps or local services that block VPN traffic. ### Multi-hop (Double VPN) Mozilla VPN's multi-hop feature routes your traffic through two server locations for an additional layer of encryption. You choose both the entry and exit servers, giving you control over the routing path. This is a genuine privacy upgrade for high-risk situations — journalists, activists, or users in surveillance-heavy countries. Performance takes a hit with multi-hop enabled: expect 40-60% speed reduction compared to single-hop connections. But for users who prioritize privacy over speed, the trade-off is worthwhile. ### DNS-Level Blocking Mozilla VPN includes built-in blocking for ads, trackers, and malware at the DNS level. This works across all apps on your device, not just the browser. In our testing, it blocked most common ad networks and known malicious domains effectively, though it is not as comprehensive as a dedicated ad blocker like uBlock Origin. ## Mozilla VPN vs Mullvad vs ProtonVPN Since Mozilla VPN is built on Mullvad's infrastructure, and ProtonVPN is the closest competitor in the privacy-focused space, here is how they compare head-to-head: | Feature | Mozilla VPN | Mullvad | ProtonVPN Plus | |---------|------------|---------|---------------| | **Price (best)** | €4.99/mo (annual) | €5.00/mo (flat) | €4.49/mo (2-year) | | **Price (monthly)** | €9.99/mo | €5.00/mo | €9.99/mo | | **Servers** | 500+ | 696 | 9,200+ | | **Countries** | 30+ | 45+ | 112 | | **Devices** | 5 | 5 | 10 | | **Protocol** | WireGuard | WireGuard | WireGuard, OpenVPN, Stealth | | **Kill Switch** | Yes | Yes (always on) | Yes | | **Split Tunneling** | Win/Android/Linux | Win/Android/Linux | All platforms | | **Multi-hop** | Yes | Yes | Yes (Secure Core) | | **Netflix** | Inconsistent | No | Yes (30+ libraries) | | **Open Source** | Yes | Yes | Yes | | **Audits** | 2 (Cure53) | 8 (multiple firms) | 5+ (Securitum, others) | | **Anonymous Signup** | No (email required) | Yes (number only) | No (email required) | | **Anonymous Payment** | No | Yes (cash, Monero) | Bitcoin accepted | | **Jurisdiction** | USA (Five Eyes) | Sweden (14 Eyes) | Switzerland | | **Free Tier** | No | No | Yes (limited) | | **Ad Blocking** | Yes | Yes | Yes (NetShield) | | **Tor Integration** | No | Yes (SOCKS5) | Yes (Tor servers) | ### The Verdict on Alternatives **Choose Mullvad** if you want the same network Mozilla VPN uses, with more features, transparent pricing, and genuinely anonymous signup. Mullvad is the better product at the same or lower price. **Choose ProtonVPN** if you want a privacy-focused VPN that also excels at streaming, offers Swiss jurisdiction, and has the largest server network of the three. ProtonVPN Plus is the most well-rounded option. **Choose Mozilla VPN** if you specifically value Mozilla's brand, want the simplest possible VPN app, or already subscribe to Mozilla's privacy bundle that includes VPN, Relay, and Monitor. ## What I Don't Like About Mozilla VPN Let me be direct about the negatives — there are several that potential buyers should weigh carefully. ### 1. The Mullvad Markup Problem Mozilla VPN is a reskinned version of Mullvad's network. On the monthly plan, you pay €9.99 for what Mullvad sells for €5.00. Even on the annual plan, you are paying essentially the same price (€4.99 vs €5.00) but with fewer features. Mullvad gives you SOCKS5 proxy, anonymous account creation, cash payment, and more server locations. Mozilla VPN gives you a prettier app and the Mozilla name. For privacy-focused users — the exact target audience — Mullvad is objectively the better deal. ### 2. Tiny Server Network 500+ servers in 30+ countries is below average for a paid VPN in 2026. NordVPN has 6,800+, Surfshark has 3,200+, even ProtonVPN offers 9,200+. A smaller network means more congestion during peak hours and fewer options for finding a fast, nearby server. ### 3. No Anonymous Payment For a VPN that markets itself on privacy and is backed by a privacy-advocacy organization, the lack of cryptocurrency or cash payment is a glaring omission. You need a credit card, debit card, or PayPal account — all of which tie your real identity to your VPN subscription. Mullvad and ProtonVPN both accept Bitcoin; Mullvad accepts Monero and literal cash. ### 4. Inconsistent Streaming The streaming situation is worse than a clean "no" — it is unpredictable. Some reviewers report Netflix working fine; others report complete blocking. This inconsistency means you cannot rely on Mozilla VPN for streaming, but you also cannot plan around its limitations with certainty. ### 5. iOS and macOS Limitations No split tunneling on Apple platforms removes a key feature for a significant portion of users. When competitors like ProtonVPN offer split tunneling on all platforms, this gap is hard to justify. ### 6. US Jurisdiction While Mozilla's infrastructure sits on Mullvad's Swedish-hosted servers, the company itself operates under US law. The US can compel companies to provide data and issue gag orders preventing disclosure. Whether this matters practically depends on how much data Mozilla handles beyond the VPN tunnel — but it is a philosophical concern for privacy purists. ## Who Should Choose Mozilla VPN? ### The Mozilla Loyalist If you already use Firefox, have a Mozilla account, and value what Mozilla stands for as an organization, their VPN is a natural extension of that ecosystem. The Firefox browser extension on Windows adds per-site VPN controls that no other VPN offers. ### The VPN Beginner Mozilla VPN's app is genuinely one of the easiest to use. No configuration needed, no protocol choices to understand, no server optimization. Turn it on, pick a country, done. For someone buying their first VPN, this simplicity has real value. ### The Privacy Bundle Buyer Mozilla offers a broader privacy protection plan at $8.25/month that includes VPN, Firefox Relay Premium (email masking), and Mozilla Monitor (data breach alerts). If you want all three services, the bundle may offer better value than buying them separately. ### Everyone Else: Look Elsewhere For streamers, power users, large households, or anyone who wants the best price-to-feature ratio, NordVPN, Surfshark, ProtonVPN, or even Mullvad itself are all stronger choices. Mozilla VPN is a good product in a category full of great ones. ## FAQ **Is Mozilla VPN safe to use in 2026?** Yes. Mozilla VPN has been audited twice by Cure53, an independent security firm, with all identified vulnerabilities fixed and reports published publicly. The VPN runs on Mullvad's infrastructure, which has undergone eight additional audits by multiple firms. The apps are fully open source and available on GitHub for independent verification. Our testing found zero DNS, WebRTC, or IPv6 leaks. **Is Mozilla VPN the same as Mullvad VPN?** Not exactly, but close. Mozilla VPN leases its entire server network from Mullvad and uses the same WireGuard protocol. However, Mozilla VPN has its own apps, its own pricing structure, its own customer support, and its own feature set. Think of it as a Mozilla-branded frontend running on Mullvad's backend. Mullvad offers more features (SOCKS5 proxy, anonymous accounts, cash payment) at a comparable or lower price. **Does Mozilla VPN work with Netflix?** Results are inconsistent. Some users and reviewers report successful Netflix unblocking on specific US, UK, and Japanese servers, while others find Netflix blocked across all servers. We do not recommend Mozilla VPN if streaming is a primary use case. NordVPN and Surfshark are far more reliable for Netflix access. **How many devices can I use with Mozilla VPN?** Mozilla VPN allows up to 5 simultaneous device connections. You can install the app on any number of devices, but only 5 can be connected at the same time. This is lower than NordVPN (10 devices), ProtonVPN Plus (10 devices), and Surfshark (unlimited devices). **Does Mozilla VPN have a free version?** No. Mozilla VPN does not offer a free tier or free trial. The cheapest option is the annual plan at €4.99 per month (€59.88 billed annually). There is a 30-day money-back guarantee for first-time customers if you want to test it risk-free. ProtonVPN offers a free tier with limited servers if you want a no-cost privacy VPN. **Does Mozilla VPN work in China?** Mozilla VPN does not have dedicated obfuscation features designed for bypassing the Great Firewall of China. While WireGuard traffic may occasionally pass through, there are no Shadowsocks bridges, no stealth protocols, and no specific tooling for restrictive networks. If you need a VPN that works in China, consider Mullvad (with bridge mode), NordVPN (with obfuscated servers), or ExpressVPN. **Is Mozilla VPN better than a free VPN?** Significantly better. Free VPNs typically monetize through data collection, ad injection, or bandwidth selling — all of which undermine the privacy you are trying to protect. Mozilla VPN uses audited infrastructure, collects no browsing logs, and is backed by a non-profit-driven organization with a 25-year track record on internet privacy. If you cannot afford a paid VPN, ProtonVPN's free tier is a safer alternative to random free VPN apps. **Why does Mozilla VPN cost more than Mullvad if it uses Mullvad's servers?** Mozilla adds its own app development, customer support, brand trust, and simplified user experience on top of Mullvad's infrastructure. The monthly plan at €9.99 includes this Mozilla premium. On the annual plan, the effective price (€4.99/month) is nearly identical to Mullvad's €5.00/month. Whether the Mozilla experience justifies the price depends on how much you value app simplicity and brand familiarity versus Mullvad's additional features and anonymous signup. ## Sources

sources.title: Gizmodo - Mozilla VPN Review 2026: Is It Safe and Up to the Promises?
